title: Issue Volume Anomaly Detection
dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:issue` from the `issue` modular input in `TA_cisco_catalyst` (Splunkbase 7538). Same data feed as UC-5.13.21. Requires **7–30 days of historical data** for the `eventstats` baseline. Uses `dc(issueId)` per time bucket for unique issue volume.
spl:
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" status!="RESOLVED"
| bin _time span=4h
| stats dc(issueId) as issue_count by _time
| eventstats avg(issue_count) as baseline stdev(issue_count) as stdev_issues
| where issue_count > (baseline + 2*stdev_issues) AND stdev_issues > 0
| eval deviation=round((issue_count-baseline)/stdev_issues,1)
| sort -deviation
description: Applies statistical anomaly detection to the volume of active Assurance issues, flagging time periods where the issue count spiked significantly above its historical baseline — catching correlated failure events that individual issue alerts miss because each individual issue looks like a normal P3.
value: A P1 alert (UC-5.13.23) catches one critical issue. This UC catches 50 P3 issues appearing simultaneously — which collectively represent a major event that no single P3 alert would trigger. When a firmware push goes wrong and 30 switches simultaneously report 'Configuration Non-Compliance,' no individual issue is P1, but the volume spike is unmistakable. This UC detects correlated failure events by their statistical signature: an issue count that's unusually high compared to your network's normal volume. It's the difference between 'we have a few more issues than usual' and 'something systemic just happened.'
implementation: Same `issue` input as UC-5.13.21. Schedule as twice-daily report (not real-time alert). Use 14-day lookback for stable baselines. Focus on upward anomalies (`issue_count > baseline + 2σ`) — unlike health scores, higher issue counts are always bad.
visualization: (1) Table: anomalous time buckets with issue_count, baseline, deviation — sorted by deviation descending. (2) Line chart: issue_count over the search window with shaded band for baseline ± 2σ. Points above the upper band are anomalies. (3) Single value: count of anomalous time periods in the last 7 days. (4) Drilldown: click an anomalous bucket → filter UC-5.13.21 to that time range to see which specific issues appeared.

references.title: Catalyst Center Intent API — Issues endpoint

knownFalsePositives:
**Catalyst Center upgrade adding new issue detection capabilities.** After upgrading Catalyst Center, the Assurance engine may detect issue types it previously missed, creating a legitimate spike in issue volume that's not a network degradation event. Distinguish by checking whether new issue `name` values appeared coincident with the upgrade. Suppress by annotating the chart with upgrade dates and allowing a 48-hour stabilisation window.

**Scheduled maintenance causing a correlated issue burst.** A firmware push affecting many devices simultaneously generates issues (Device Unreachable, Config Non-Compliant) that spike the volume. Distinguish by correlating with ITSM change records or `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs"` for SWIM activity. Suppress with `catalyst_maintenance_windows` lookup.

**Issue volume naturally higher during business hours.** More clients connected during business hours → more potential issues detected. If the baseline doesn't account for time-of-day patterns, business-hour volumes may appear anomalous compared to a flat 24-hour average. Suppress by using `| eventstats avg(issue_count) as baseline by date_hour` for hour-of-day-aware baselines.

**Single high-impact event generating many correlated issues.** An upstream router failure may cause Catalyst Center to generate 30 related issues (each downstream switch reports unhealthy, each client reports onboarding failure). This is a real event — do not suppress. But note that the root cause is one device, not 30.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• UC-5.13.21 (Issue Summary) and UC-5.13.22 (Issue Trending) must be operational — same `issue` data feed.
• **Minimum 7 days of historical data** for meaningful baselines. 14 days recommended.
• This UC detects **upward** anomalies (issue spikes) rather than downward anomalies. A lower-than-normal issue count is good, not anomalous. The `where issue_count > (baseline + 2σ)` uses `>` (above baseline), not `<` (below baseline) as in UC-5.13.20.
• This is a **run-tier** analytical view for twice-daily engineering review, not a real-time paging alert.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same `issue` input as UC-5.13.21. No additional configuration.

Step 2 — Create the search and report
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" status!="RESOLVED"
| bin _time span=4h
| stats dc(issueId) as issue_count by _time
| eventstats avg(issue_count) as baseline stdev(issue_count) as stdev_issues
| where issue_count > (baseline + 2*stdev_issues) AND stdev_issues > 0
| eval deviation=round((issue_count-baseline)/stdev_issues,1)
| sort -deviation
```

Why `status != "RESOLVED"`: counts only active issues. Including resolved issues would measure total issue churn rather than active backlog size.

Why `dc(issueId)` not `count`: `count` inflates with poll frequency. `dc(issueId)` per time bucket gives the actual number of unique active issues in that period.

Why `span=4h`: balances detail with noise. At 1-hour span, natural intra-day variation may produce frequent false anomalies. At 1-day span, short spikes are averaged out. 4-hour buckets capture meaningful multi-hour events while smoothing within-hour noise.

Why `> (baseline + 2σ)` not `< (baseline - 2σ)`: for issue volume, ABOVE normal is the problem. Below-normal volume is good. This is the opposite of health-score anomaly detection (UC-5.13.20) where BELOW normal is the problem.

Why `stdev_issues > 0` guard: networks with perfectly constant issue counts (stdev = 0) would false-fire on any variation.

Schedule as Report: cron `0 7,19 * * *` (twice daily), time range `-14d to now`.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Run the search over the last 14 days. You should see 0–5 anomalous time periods. If > 20, sigma is too low or the baseline window is too short.

(b) Cross-reference: pick an anomalous period. Open UC-5.13.22 (Issue Trending) for that same time range. You should see a visible spike in the timechart.

(c) Known incident check: if a firmware push or major event occurred last week, the anomaly detection should flag the corresponding time period.

(d) Baseline stability: compare results for 7-day vs 14-day windows. The same spikes should appear in both.

(e) Drilldown: for the top anomaly, run `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" status!="RESOLVED" earliest=<bucket_start> latest=<bucket_end> | stats dc(issueId) by name, category | sort -dc(issueId)`. This shows WHAT issues caused the spike.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard placement (on the "Advanced Analytics" or "Network Anomaly" dashboard):
• Table of anomalous time periods with deviation scores.
• Line chart: issue_count over search window with baseline ± 2σ band. Points above the upper band are highlighted.
• Drilldown: click a spike → show the top issue names from that period.

Interpretation:
• **High deviation (> 4σ)**: major correlated event. Check UC-5.13.21 for the dominant issue category and UC-5.13.26 for the affected devices/sites.
• **Moderate deviation (2–4σ)**: elevated issue volume. May be a minor change impact or a growing trend.
• **Same time-of-day appearing across multiple days**: recurring correlated event (e.g., backup window causing interface flaps, DHCP lease expiry wave).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Every business day is flagged** — baseline doesn't account for time-of-day patterns. Use `| eventstats avg(issue_count) as baseline by date_hour` for hour-aware baselines.

• **No anomalies ever detected** — issue volume is very stable (good!), or sigma is too high. Try 1.5σ.

• **Anomaly after every push to main** — your deployment pipeline may cause transient issues. Annotate deployment times.

• **`stdev_issues` is very small, causing false anomalies** — the issue count barely varies, so even +1 issue triggers the anomaly. Add `| where issue_count > baseline + 5` as a minimum-delta guard alongside the sigma check.

• **Drilldown shows all issues are P4 informational** — the spike is from Assurance adding new informational detections, not from network degradation. Filter to `| where priority IN ("P1","P2","P3")` for operational anomaly detection.

• **Search returns different results depending on time picker** — expected. The baseline shifts with the search window. Use a fixed 14-day window for consistent results.

• **Issue count in Splunk ≠ Catalyst Center** — the GUI shows the current snapshot; this search shows historical unique issue counts per time bucket. They measure different things.

• **Performance concerns** — the `issue` sourcetype is moderate volume (~1.7 MB/day for 30 issues). A 14-day `eventstats` window processes ~84 buckets — negligible compute.
grandmaExplanation: We use statistics to spot when the number of network problems suddenly spikes above what is normal for your network. A flood of 30 problems appearing at once usually means something big went wrong — like a bad software update or a power failure — even if each individual problem looks minor by itself.
